cue: How do you say someone is out of their mind?

es.literal: for someone to be missing a screw
es.meaning: To be a bit crazy; to have something not quite right upstairs.
es.example: ¿Renunciar sin otro trabajo? Le falta un tornillo.
es.exampleTranslation: Quit with no other job? He's got a screw loose.

it.meaning: To be a bit crazy; to be not all there.
it.example: Lasciare tutto così? Gli manca un venerdì.
it.exampleTranslation: Drop everything like that? He's not all there.

bridge.note: Madness as a missing piece — but of what? Spanish removes hardware: a screw gone from the machine of the mind, something mechanical come loose. Italian removes a DAY: gli manca un venerdì, he's short a Friday — from Robinson Crusoe's Friday, or from the idea that a person missing a day of the week has lost part of their very calendar. A machine with a loose part versus a week with a hole in it.

interference.es_to_it: Screws are not the Italian image; the madman is short a weekday — gli manca un venerdì. (Also common: gli manca una rotella, a little wheel.)
interference.it_to_es: 'Le falta un viernes' means nothing in Spanish; the missing part is a screw — un tornillo.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. Screw-vs-Friday. NOTE: IT 'manca una rotella' (a cog) is the closer mechanical twin — reviewer may prefer it, in which case this re-pairs. Second madness entry (0002 balcone is different).
